title: Second Treatise of Government
author: Locke, John
summary: Locke argues against patriarchal and divine-right theories of monarchy, asserting political power derives from consent. He defines the state of nature as one of freedom and equality governed by natural law and reason, obliging preservation of life, liberty, and property. Men possess a right to enforce that law by punishing transgressors. Civil government arises by mutual agreement to protect property through laws and impartial judges. Governments must exercise authority for the public good; illegitimate rulers may be resisted. Locke targets Sir Robert Filmer’s claims, defends voluntary consent as the basis of legitimate sovereignty, and justifies the Glorious Revolution peaceably attained.

characters.name: Adam
characters.personality: referenced as the alleged first proprietor in patriarchal theory
characters.lifespan: biblical figure; discussed as theoretical origin of claimed authority rather than an active participant

characters.name: Sir Robert Filmer
characters.personality: authoritative, patriarchalist (as portrayed by Locke); defender of paternal and divine-right monarchy
characters.lifespan: represented as the primary target of Locke's refutation; his doctrine is criticized and overthrown in the text

characters.name: King William
characters.age: not specified (William III, contemporary to Locke)
characters.personality: presented favorably as the restored monarch justified by consent
characters.lifespan: celebrated in the Preface as the lawful king whose title Locke seeks to establish

characters.name: Richard Hooker
characters.personality: quoted approvingly for views on equality and mutual duty
characters.lifespan: cited as authoritative moral commentator within Locke's argument

characters.name: Hobbs
characters.personality: mentioned among writers Locke engages with; typifies competing views
characters.lifespan: referenced in editorial notes and Observations; not a participant in the narrative

characters.name: Milton
characters.personality: referenced among authors addressed in Observations
characters.lifespan: mentioned in passing, used as a point of contrast in commentary

characters.name: Mr. Peter Coste
characters.personality: recipient of Locke’s last corrected copy
characters.lifespan: noted as the person to whom Locke delivered his corrected copy; mentioned in editorial note

characters.name: C. B. McPherson
characters.personality: editor and modern commentator (appears in front matter)
characters.lifespan: named as editor of a later edition; referenced in the digitization notes

characters.name: People of England
characters.personality: described as lovers of just and natural rights and as the consenting body
characters.lifespan: treated collectively as the political community whose consent legitimizes government

characters.name: Editor (unnamed)
characters.personality: annotator and publisher of the edition (offers notes and collation)
characters.lifespan: appears in prefatory material as responsible for collating editions and noting corrections
